issues.original: Most people quit Vim because they try to memorize shortcuts. That is the wrong frame. Vim is a *small language*: learn the grammar — **operator + motion** — and you can express any edit without ever opening a cheat sheet again. This guide walks you through the 80% of Vim you will use daily, then shows how the remaining 20% composes naturally from the same handful of rules.
issues.fixed: Most people quit Vim because they try to memorize shortcuts. That's the wrong approach. Vim is a *small language*: learn the grammar — **operator + motion** — and you can express any edit without ever opening a cheat sheet. This guide covers the 80% of Vim you'll use daily and shows how the remaining 20% naturally follows from the same basic rules.

issues.original: Vim revolves around one loop: 1. **Move** to what you want to change (a *motion*). 2. **Select** the scope (implicitly via the motion, or explicitly in Visual mode). 3. **Apply** an *operator* — delete, change, yank. Once that loop becomes reflex, you stop 'remembering keys' and start 'speaking Vim.'
issues.fixed: Vim revolves around a simple loop: 1. **Move** to what you want to change (a *motion*). 2. **Select** the scope (implicitly via the motion or explicitly in Visual mode). 3. **Apply** an *operator* — delete, change, yank. Once this loop becomes second nature, you stop 'remembering keys' and start 'speaking Vim.'

issues.original: The single most important habit:** the moment you finish typing, press [CODE]. Idle time should be spent in Normal mode, where every key is a command.
issues.fixed: The single most important habit: the moment you finish typing, press [CODE]. Spend idle time in Normal mode, where every key is a command.

issues.original: Now compose: - [CODE] — delete a word - [CODE] — delete to end of line - [CODE] — change inner word - [CODE] — yank a paragraph - [CODE] — delete the next three words
issues.fixed: Now, compose: - [CODE] — delete a word - [CODE] — delete to end of line - [CODE] — change inner word - [CODE] — yank a paragraph - [CODE] — delete the next three words

issues.original: Two motions deserve special mention because they pair perfectly with operators: - [CODE] jumps to the next occurrence of [CODE] on the line; [CODE] jumps to just before it. So [CODE] deletes everything up to and including the next comma — a very common need when editing argument lists. - [CODE] jumps between matching brackets. [CODE] from an opening [CODE] deletes the entire parenthesised expression.
issues.fixed: Two motions are especially useful because they pair well with operators: - [CODE] jumps to the next occurrence of [CODE] on the line; [CODE] jumps to just before it. So [CODE] deletes everything up to and including the next comma — a common need when editing argument lists. - [CODE] jumps between matching brackets. [CODE] from an opening [CODE] deletes the entire parenthesized expression.

issues.original: The difficulty of Linux rarely lies in the commands themselves. The hard part is having a clear *map* of the system: why it dominates servers, what multi-user and per-file permissions offer, what changes when switching between Debian and Red Hat lineages, and what to do in the first ten minutes after an SSH prompt opens.
issues.fixed: The difficulty with Linux rarely lies in the commands themselves. The hard part is having a clear *map* of the system: why it dominates servers, what multi-user and per-file permissions offer, what changes when switching between Debian and Red Hat lineages, and what to do in the first ten minutes after an SSH prompt opens.

issues.original: This post is the **entry guide** for the entire Linux series. It first builds the mental model — philosophy, distributions, the FHS tree — and then walks you through the commands you will use ten times an hour: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], plus pipelines, redirection, SSH, and a quick taste of permissions and processes.

It first builds the mental model — philosophy, distributions, the FHS tree — and then walks you through the commands you'll use ten times an hour: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], plus pipelines, redirection, SSH, and a quick taste of permissions and processes.

issues.original: Each topic is intentionally **kept short**; deeper details are in the dedicated articles (File Permissions, Disk Management, User Management, Service Management, Process Management, Package Management, Advanced File Operations).
issues.fixed: Each topic is intentionally **kept short**; deeper details are in the dedicated articles (File Permissions, Disk Management, User Management, Service Management, Process Management, Package Management, Advanced File Operations).

issues.original: Three design decisions explain most Linux quirks a newcomer notices: it was built for **multiple users at once**, it treats **files as the universal interface**, and it prioritizes **automation over clicking**.
issues.fixed: Three design decisions explain most Linux quirks a newcomer notices: it was built for **multiple users at once**, it treats **files as the universal interface**, and it prioritizes **automation over clicking**.

issues.original: - **Open and customisable.** Every component, from the kernel to the init system, can be swapped, recompiled, or stripped down. Both a 5 MB Alpine container and a 12 GB Oracle Linux install call themselves Linux.
issues.fixed: - **Open and customizable.** Every component, from the kernel to the init system, can be swapped, recompiled, or stripped down. Both a 5 MB Alpine container and a 12 GB Oracle Linux install call themselves Linux.

issues.original: - **Stable enough to forget about.** Production servers routinely run for years without a reboot. The [CODE] command on a long-lived machine printing [CODE] is a normal sight, not a brag.
issues.fixed: - **Stable enough to forget about.** Production servers routinely run for years without a reboot. The [CODE] command on a long-lived machine printing [CODE] is a normal sight, not a brag.

issues.original: Most distributions descend from a few ancestors. The package manager is usually the quickest way to tell them apart.

issues.original: #### 1. Multi-user, multi-task
issues.fixed: #### 1. Multi-user, Multi-task

issues.original: Dozens of users may log in simultaneously over SSH or local TTYs, each running many processes in parallel. The kernel must isolate their CPU time, memory, files, and network sockets. This is why the permission model is strict: without it, any user could read another user's secrets or kill their processes.
issues.fixed: Dozens of users may log in simultaneously over SSH or local TTYs, each running many processes in parallel. The kernel must isolate their CPU time, memory, files, and network sockets. This is why the permission model is strict: without it, any user could read another user's secrets or kill their processes.

issues.original: Most people learn package management as three commands: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. That works until something goes wrong - a dependency conflict, an upgrade that won't apply, a kernel that doesn't boot, a mirror that times out from inside China. At that point you need a model of what is actually happening: what a *package* contains, what the *manager* is solving for, where it stores state, and how the difference between Debian's [CODE] and Red Hat's [CODE] shows up at 2 a.m. on a production box.

This works until something goes wrong—a dependency conflict, an upgrade that won't apply, a kernel that doesn't boot, or a mirror that times out in China. At that point, you need to understand what's actually happening: what a *package* contains, what the *manager* is solving for, where it stores state, and how the differences between Debian's [CODE] and Red Hat's [CODE] show up at 2 a.m. on a production box.

issues.original: This article is both the model and the cookbook. We start with what's in a [CODE] or [CODE] file and why a manager is necessary. Then we walk through [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE] side by side—not just 'the equivalent commands' but where they share assumptions and where they diverge (dependency resolution, version pinning, downgrade, repo trust). After that, we cover the things you only learn the hard way: configuring a domestic mirror that works, building Nginx from source when the repo version is too old, adding a binary tarball like the JDK without [CODE], and a few habits that keep production boxes upgradable for years.

A package is a single file ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE]) that contains everything one piece of software needs *plus* metadata describing how it should be installed. The metadata is what makes a package a package - without it you have a tarball.
issues.fixed: A package is a single file ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE]) that contains everything a piece of software needs, plus metadata describing how it should be installed. The metadata is what makes a package a package—without it, you have a tarball.

issues.original: On top of those payloads, the package metadata records: the package name and version, dependencies ([CODE] in [CODE], [CODE] in [CODE]), conflicts, post-install scripts (e.g. [CODE]), and a SHA256 of every file. That last bit is what lets [CODE] or [CODE] later tell you which files have been tampered with.
issues.fixed: In addition to these payloads, the package metadata includes: the package name and version, dependencies ([CODE] in [CODE], [CODE] in [CODE]), conflicts, post-install scripts (e.g., [CODE]), and a SHA256 hash of every file. This last part allows [CODE] or [CODE] to later identify which files have been tampered with.

issues.original: Without one you would, for every piece of software: 1. Find a download link (and trust whoever runs the site). 2. Manually resolve dependencies - A needs B 1.1+, B needs C, C conflicts with D you already installed. 3. Decide where each file goes. 4. Remember every file you copied so you can uninstall it later. 5. Poll upstream by hand for security updates.
issues.fixed: Without a package manager, for every piece of software, you would: 1. Find a download link (and trust whoever runs the site). 2. Manually resolve dependencies—A needs B 1.1+, B needs C, C conflicts with D, which you already installed. 3. Decide where each file goes. 4. Remember every file you copied so you can uninstall it later. 5. Poll upstream by hand for security updates.

issues.original: The biggest productivity jump on Linux is not memorising more commands. It is learning to **compose small tools** into clean data flows.
issues.fixed: The biggest productivity boost on Linux isn't memorizing more commands. It's learning to **compose small tools** into clean data flows.

issues.original: Every Linux process starts life with three open file descriptors. Understanding them is the prerequisite for understanding everything else in this article.
issues.fixed: Every Linux process starts with three open file descriptors. Understanding them is key to grasping the rest of this article.

issues.original: A tiny demonstration: Only stdout was captured into [CODE]; stderr went straight to the terminal because we did not redirect it.
issues.fixed: A small demonstration: Only stdout was captured into [CODE]; stderr went straight to the terminal because it wasn't redirected.

issues.original: You can inspect a live process's open fds via [CODE]: Every redirection and pipe you write in the shell ultimately boils down to **rewiring those small integers** before the program starts.
issues.fixed: You can inspect a live process's open file descriptors via [CODE]. Every redirection and pipe you write in the shell ultimately boils down to **rewiring those small integers** before the program starts.

issues.original: The traditional form has an order trap that bites everybody once: Read it left to right and remember that [CODE] means 'make fd 2 point wherever fd 1 currently points' — not 'merge fd 2 into fd 1'.
issues.fixed: The traditional form has an order trap that catches everyone at least once: read it left to right and remember that [CODE] means 'make fd 2 point wherever fd 1 currently points' — not 'merge fd 2 into fd 1'.

issues.original: Three reasons this style wins: No intermediate files. Data flows in memory; nothing to clean up. Streaming. Output starts appearing as soon as the producer writes its first line; you do not wait for the whole file. Composable. Each stage is independently testable and substitutable.
issues.fixed: Three reasons this style wins: No intermediate files. Data flows in memory, so there's nothing to clean up. Streaming. Output appears as soon as the producer writes its first line; you don't wait for the whole file. Composable. Each stage is independently testable and substitutable.

issues.original: A "service" on Linux is a long-running background process whose job is to be there when something needs it: synchronise the clock, listen for SSH connections, accept HTTP requests, run a backup at 3 AM. You almost never start one of these by hand. Something has to start them at boot, restart them when they crash, capture their logs, decide what depends on what, and shut everything down cleanly when the machine powers off. On every modern distribution that something is **systemd**.
issues.fixed: A "service" on Linux is a long-running background process that's ready when needed: synchronize the clock, listen for SSH connections, accept HTTP requests, or run a backup at 3 AM. You rarely start these manually. They need to be started at boot, restarted if they crash, have their logs captured, and dependencies managed. When the machine powers off, everything must shut down cleanly. On modern distributions, **systemd** handles this.

issues.original: This article is the working model I wish someone had handed me when I first got dropped into a production server. We start from why a dedicated service manager exists at all, build up the unit/target mental model, walk through the [CODE] commands you actually use day to day, dissect a unit file line by line, and then cover the adjacent surfaces: [CODE] for logs, [CODE] units as the modern cron, and a step-by-step playbook for "the service won't start."
issues.fixed: This article is the working model I wish I had when I first worked on a production server. It starts with why a dedicated service manager is necessary, builds the unit/target mental model, walks through the [CODE] commands you use daily, dissects a unit file line by line, and covers related topics: [CODE] for logs, [CODE] units as the modern cron, and a step-by-step playbook for when a service won't start.

issues.original: A bare Linux kernel knows how to load drivers, mount the root filesystem, and execute exactly one program (PID 1). Everything else is somebody else's problem. Historically that "somebody" was **SysV init**: a small program that read scripts out of [CODE] and ran them, in alphabetical order, one after another. Each script was a hand-rolled shell program responsible for forking the daemon into the background, writing a PID file, and pretending to know whether the service was actually up.
issues.fixed: A bare Linux kernel can load drivers, mount the root filesystem, and execute one program (PID 1). Everything else is someone else's problem. Historically, that "someone" was **SysV init**: a small program that read scripts from [CODE] and ran them in alphabetical order. Each script was a custom shell program that forked the daemon into the background, wrote a PID file, and tried to determine if the service was running.

issues.original: That approach worked, but it accumulated three structural problems: 1. **Serial startup is slow.** Running 80 init scripts sequentially on a modern server wastes most of a multi-core CPU just waiting on I/O. 2. **Dependencies live in the scripts themselves.** If [CODE] needs the network up first, you encode that by hoping the alphabet cooperates ([CODE] before [CODE]) and by sprinkling [CODE] calls through the scripts. It is exactly as fragile as it sounds. 3. **There is no shared concept of state.** Each daemon decides for itself how to background, where to write its PID, how to log, how to restart on crash. The init system has no way to ask "is sshd actually running?" beyond "did this script exit 0?"
issues.fixed: That approach worked, but it had three structural problems: 1. **Serial startup is slow.** Running 80 init scripts sequentially on a modern server wastes most of a multi-core CPU waiting on I/O. 2. **Dependencies are in the scripts.** If [CODE] needs the network, you hope the alphabet works ([CODE] before [CODE]) and sprinkle [CODE] calls through the scripts. This is as fragile as it sounds. 3. **No shared state.** Each daemon decides how to background, where to write its PID, how to log, and how to restart. The init system can only check if a script exited with 0.

issues.original: systemd replaces all of that with a uniform supervisor that knows the difference between "the start command returned" and "the service is ready," parallelises whatever it can, and keeps a structured log of everything that happens.
issues.fixed: systemd replaces all of that with a uniform supervisor that distinguishes between "the start command returned" and "the service is ready," parallelizes tasks, and maintains a structured log of everything that happens.

issues.original: If you only ever ran [CODE] and [CODE] on a single laptop, you can probably get away without thinking about any of this. The moment more than one human (or more than one service) shares a host, "user management" stops being paperwork and starts being the security model: it decides who can log in, which UID owns the files a process writes, which commands [CODE] will lift to root, and how long a stolen password remains useful.
issues.fixed: If you only ever run [CODE] and [CODE] on a single laptop, you can probably ignore all of this. When more than one person or service shares a host, user management becomes a security model. It determines who can log in, which UID owns the files a process writes, which commands [CODE] elevates to root, and how long a stolen password remains useful.

issues.original: This is the part most tutorials get muddled. Every account has exactly **one primary group** (the GID in [CODE]) and **zero or more supplementary groups** (rows of [CODE] whose member list contains this user).
issues.fixed: This is where most tutorials get confusing. Each account has exactly **one primary group** (the GID in [CODE]) and **zero or more supplementary groups** (rows in [CODE] that list this user as a member).

issues.original: A locked account still exists, still owns its files, and can still be the target of [CODE] from root. It just can't be authenticated against by password. This is exactly what you want when an employee leaves but their files might still be needed for a few weeks.
issues.fixed: A locked account still exists, still owns its files, and can still be the target of [CODE] from root. It just can't be authenticated with a password. This is ideal when an employee leaves but their files are still needed for a few weeks.
